content.full_text:
Com m issio11, 
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Administratian 
322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322-5901 
Enfarcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-6444 
April 8, 1982 
Cheriel Jensen 
13737 Quito Road 
Saratoga, CA 
95070 
Re: 
Advice Letter No. A-82-060 
Dear Ms. Jensen: 
~hank you for your telephone call requesting advice from our 
office. 
As you stated to me, you are currently serving as a city 
councilmember for the City of Saratoga and you intend to run 
again for this position in the next election. 
You are also employed 
by the county as a land use planner. 
Your question is whether 
the Political Reform Act prevents you from serving in both capacities. 
The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from being 
both a city councilmember and a county planner. 
Rather, it deals 
with your decision-making in both positions and requires you to 
comply with its disclosure and disqualification provisions. 
with respect to the disqualification provisions, a public 
official is forbidden from making, participating in the making 
of, or in any way attempting to use her official position to 
influence a governmental decision in which she knows or has reason 
to kn9~ she has a financial interest (Government Code Section 
87100~). 
Both of your positions are subject to this prohibition, 
for the term "public official" includes members, officers and 
employees of state and local government agencies. 
(See Section 
82048.) 
In either position you must disqualify yourself from partici-
pating in any decision in which it is reasonably f027seeable that 
the decision will have a material financ~ql effect,~ distinguishable 
from its effect on the public generally,_1 on: 
1 
All statutory references 
unles~/otherwise noted. 
£ 
See the enclosed copy of 
Code Section 18702 for the meaning 
made are to the Government Code 
2 California Administrative 
"material financial effect." 
3 
See 
enclosed copy of 2 California Ad 
nistrative 
Section 18703 
r more explanation 
the "public generally" 
exception. 


Cheriel Jensen 
April 8, 1982 
Page TNO 
(a) 
any business entity in which you have a direct or 
indirect investment worth more than $1,000; 
(b) 
any real property interest in which you have a 
direct or indirect interest worth more than $1,000; 
(c) 
any source of income aggregating $250 or more, 
received by or promised to the public official within 12 
months prior to when the decision is made; or 
(d) 
any business entity in which the public official 
is a 
di:e~tor, officer, par4;er, trustee, employee, or holds 
any posItIon of management.-
In performing your jobs, you must consider whether the decisions 
you are confronted with will have a reasonably foreseeable financial 
effect on one of the above financial interests so as to require 
disqualification. 
Please note that the salary you receive from either job is 
exempt from the Act's definition of the word "income." 
Section 
82030 states, "(b) 
Income. 
. does not include.... 
(2) 
Salary 
. received from a local. 
. government agency .... " 
Therefore, 
you may participate in decisions which may materially affect the 
income you receive from either position. 
I am including copies of two Attorney General Opinions that 
you will want to read. 
If you have any questions regarding these, 
you should contact the Attorney General's Office. 
If I can be of any further help in clarifying the Political 
Reform Act for you, please feel free to contact me. 
JSH:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
~~/ 
~ 
~t/r~(}::)/iY{~/Lh_ 
'/ 
Janis Shank fvlcLean 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
4 
vlording 
See the enclosed copy of Section 87103 for the precise 
of these provisions.

sections.question: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act prevents you from serving in both capacities.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from being both a city councilmember and a county planner. Rather, it deals with your decision-making in both positions and requires you to comply with its disclosure and disqualification provisions.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the Political Reform Act prohibits a person from simultaneously serving as a city councilmember and county land use planner.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The Act does not prohibit holding both positions, but requires compliance with disqualification provisions when decisions may have a material financial effect on specified financial interests, though salary income from either position is exempt from these requirements.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is a formal FPPC advice letter with clear question and conclusion sections. Slight OCR artifacts present (e.g., 'f027seeable' for 'foreseeably'), but core content clearly identifiable. Recipient is Cheriel Jensen; dated April 8, 1982.

QUESTION: Your question is whether the Political Reform Act prevents you from serving in both capacities.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act does not prevent you from being both a city councilmember and a county planner. Rather, it deals with your decision-making in both positions and requires you to comply with its disclosure and disqualification provisions.

embedding.summary: FPPC advises that the Political Reform Act does not prevent simultaneous service as a city councilmember and county planner, but requires disqualification from decisions affecting financial interests exceeding specified thresholds while exempting salary income from either position.
